phrasalVerbs.definition: To stop sleeping, or to cause someone to stop sleeping.
examples: I always wake up before my alarm rings.
Can you wake me up at 7 tomorrow?
The loud noise woke up the baby.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To get out of bed after waking up.
examples: I woke up at 6 but didn't get up until 7.
Get up — it's time for school!
She gets up early to go for a run.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To place clothing on your body, or to start a device or music.
examples: Put on your coat — it's cold outside.
She put on her glasses to read the menu.
Can you put on some music while we cook?

phrasalVerbs.definition: To remove clothing from your body; or (for a plane) to leave the ground.
examples: Take off your shoes before coming inside.
The plane took off on time.
He took off his hat when he entered the building.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To start a machine, light, or device.
examples: Turn on the TV — the match is starting.
Can you turn on the heater? It's freezing in here.
She turned on her laptop to check her emails.

phrasalVerbs.definition: To stop a machine, light, or device.
examples: Turn off the lights when you leave the room.
Don't forget to turn off the oven!
He turned off his phone during the film.

fillInExercises.sentence: I always ___ at 6 AM, but I stay in bed for another 30 minutes.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Wake up' means to stop sleeping. 'Get up' is to leave the bed.

fillInExercises.sentence: It's raining! You should ___ on a jacket before going out.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Put on' means to wear or place clothing on your body.

fillInExercises.sentence: Please ___ your shoes at the door — the floor is clean.
It's dark in here. Can you ___ on the lamp?
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Turn on' means to start a device or light.

fillInExercises.sentence: Don't forget to ___ the stove before you leave the kitchen.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Turn off' means to stop a device.

fillInExercises.sentence: The noise from the street ___ me up at 5 AM.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Wake up' (past: woke up) can be separated: 'woke me up'.

fillInExercises.sentence: On Saturdays, I like to stay in bed late and not ___ until noon.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Get up' means to leave the bed.

fillInExercises.sentence: The flight ___ off two hours late because of bad weather.
fillInExercises.explanation: 'Take off' (past: took off) also means for a plane to leave the ground.

lines.speaker: Jake
lines.text: Ugh… I don't want to [get up] yet.

lines.speaker: Mum
lines.text: [Put on] your uniform and come down quickly.

lines.speaker: Jake
lines.text: OK. Should I [turn off] the TV? I left it on all night.

lines.speaker: Mum
lines.text: Yes! And [take off] those pyjamas — now!

lines.speaker: Jake
lines.text: Fine. Can you [turn on] the kettle? I need a hot drink.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Did you [wake up] early this morning? The taxi is at 6.

lines.speaker: Leo
lines.text: I [get up] at 5 every day — no problem there.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Good. I need to [turn off] all the appliances before we leave.

lines.speaker: Leo
lines.text: [Put on] your warm coat — it's cold at the airport at this hour.

lines.speaker: Anna
lines.text: Right. And remember to [take off] your jacket once we board the plane.

lines.speaker: Leo
lines.text: Let me [turn on] some music for the journey — it will make time fly!
